query: neurological manifestation of unilateral spinal cord atrophy affecting dorsal columns

retrieved_docs.title: Subacute Combined Degeneration of the Spinal Cord(Archived) -- History and Physical -- Neurological Symptoms
retrieved_docs.content: Neurological symptoms may be the presenting symptoms of cobalamin deficiency and may precede the onset of hematological findings. Clinical findings are symmetrical and occur due to the involvement of the dorsal columns, spinocerebellar tracts, and lateral corticospinal tracts. In addition to spinal cord involvement, patients may also have signs and symptoms of peripheral nerve involvement, visual deficits, and neuropsychiatric disease (depression and dementia).

retrieved_docs.title: Spinal Cord Subacute Combined Degeneration -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Age younger than 50 years Short disease course Absence of sensory deficits Absence of Romberg sign Absence of Babinski signs Involvement of ≤7 spinal segments on MRI Presence of spinal cord edema Contrast enhancement of the spine Absence of spinal cord atrophy

retrieved_docs.title: Spinal Cord Subacute Combined Degeneration -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: Multifocal, diffuse demyelination, axonal loss, and myelopathic spongy vacuolation, particularly in the dorsal and lateral white matter tracts of the spinal cord, are the first changes observed in SCD of the spinal cord. Demyelinating changes predominately affect the dorsal column, lateral corticospinal tracts, and sometimes the spinothalamic tracts.

retrieved_docs.title: Spinal Cord Subacute Combined Degeneration -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Neurological signs alone in the absence of anemia have been noted.[16] The radiological evaluation includes a complete spine MRI, which will show hyperintense lesions localized to the posterior columns of the spinal cord. These lesions may be apparent in T2-weighted cervical and thoracic spinal cord images. [17] The involvement of the lateral tracts, although present clinically, rarely presents radiologically.

retrieved_docs.title: Subacute Combined Degeneration of the Spinal Cord(Archived) -- Differential Diagnosis -- Infectious Myelopathy
retrieved_docs.content: Vacuolar myelopathy can occur in patients who are human immunodeficiency virus (HIV)-positive with low CD4 counts and shares the histology, MRI findings, and symptoms of subacute combined degeneration. The myelopathy presents similarly with symmetrical involvement of the posterolateral columns. A history of HIV, low CD4 counts, opportunistic infections, acquired immunodeficiency syndrome-defining illness, and malignancy help establish a diagnosis. [33] Tabes dorsalis, a form of late neurosyphilis that damages the dorsal columns, can also present with sensory ataxia and bladder involvement. Differentiating features include dorsal root involvement, lancinating pain, and the presence of Argyll Robertson pupils.

retrieved_docs.title: Monomelic Amyotrophy (Hirayama Disease) -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Monomelic amyotrophy, previously known as Hirayama disease, is a rare, nonfamilial neurologic disorder first described by Hirayama in 1959. [1] Classical findings include unilateral or bilateral muscle atrophy and weakness of the forearms and hands without sensory loss. This condition usually progresses for 1 or 2 years before plateauing, eventually showing an abrupt arrest. [2] [3] Monomelic amyotrophy is caused by chronic ischemic changes to the anterior horn cells of the cervical spine secondary to a limited dural sac laxity. The disorder is predominantly a lower motor neuron pattern of the lesion. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Spinal Cord Subacute Combined Degeneration -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: The significant pathological changes involved in SCD of the spinal cord are multifocal, diffuse demyelination, and axonal loss, particularly in the white matter of the spinal cord. These demyelinating changes predominately affect the dorsal column, lateral corticospinal tracts, and sometimes the spinothalamic tracts. Clinical presentations include paresthesias, ataxia, sensory loss, cognitive impairment, and muscle weakness. These symptoms are collectively seen in patients with SCD of the spinal cord. [16]

retrieved_docs.title: Monomelic Amyotrophy (Hirayama Disease) -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: MRI commonly reveals a crescent-shaped lesion in the posterior epidural space of the lower cervical cord, atrophic spinal cord changes, mild asymmetric cord flattening, and increased signal intensity. Postcontrast neutral and flexion (30°-40°) MRI is the gold diagnosis standard. [26] The dynamic imaging features demonstrate a sensitivity of 70% and a specificity of 100%. These MRIs reveal a distinct anterior shift of the cervical dural sac from the lamina in the region of C4 to C7, leading to chronic microtrauma. Corresponding postcontrast enhancement of the posterior epidural venous plexus indicates ischemia. [27] Other characteristic findings include crescent-shaped postcontrast enhancement and prominent epidural flow voids due to venous dilatation during flexion. [28] [29]

retrieved_docs.title: Spinal Cord Subacute Combined Degeneration -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Investigations should cover possible dietary deficiencies and gastrointestinal causes such as Crohn or celiac disease. An evaluation that includes anti-intrinsic factor antibodies is warranted in patients with a history of autoimmune disease. [24] An MRI of the cervical or upper thoracic spine can be very valuable for patients who present with a clinical syndrome of myelopathy ie upper motor neuron pattern of weakness and/or sensory impairment of both lower extremities. The MRI typically shows bilateral symmetric T2 hyperintensity in the dorsal columns. It is often referred to as the "Inverted V"  sign. Prompt treatment results in rapid resolution of the abnormal MRI findings. [23]

retrieved_docs.title: Subacute Combined Degeneration of the Spinal Cord(Archived) -- History and Physical -- Neurological Symptoms
retrieved_docs.content: Lateral corticospinal tract dysfunction causes muscle weakness, hyperreflexia, and spasticity. Stiffness is often the initial symptom of lateral cord involvement. Diffuse hyperreflexia can occur, although ankle reflexes are usually absent. Other signs of upper motor neuron damage, such as ankle clonus and Babinski signs, may be present. Spasticity can progress to paraplegia or quadriplegia if the condition remains untreated. Sphincter involvement in advanced cases can lead to bowel and bladder incontinence.

retrieved_docs.title: Subacute Combined Degeneration of the Spinal Cord(Archived) -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Several conditions also present with involvement of the dorsal and lateral columns and can mimic the clinical and radiologic findings of subacute combined degeneration:

retrieved_docs.title: Subacute Combined Degeneration of the Spinal Cord(Archived) -- Differential Diagnosis -- Demyelinating Myelopathy
retrieved_docs.content: Transverse myelitis can cause spinal cord demyelination, but demyelination does not preferentially involve dorsal columns and is limited to 1 or 2 spinal segments. Multiple sclerosis is another cause of demyelinating lesions, although the spinal cord involvement is asymmetric and affects fewer segments. [32] Multiple sclerosis mainly affects younger patients and may be associated with other signs and symptoms (scanning speech, intention tremor, nystagmus).

retrieved_docs.title: Spinal Cord Subacute Combined Degeneration -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: HIV-positive patients with low CD4 counts share the histology, MRI findings, and symptoms of SCD. The myelopathy presents similarly with symmetrical involvement of the posterolateral columns. A history of HIV, low CD4 counts, opportunistic infections, acquired immunodeficiency syndrome-defining illness, and malignancy help establish a diagnosis. [30]

retrieved_docs.title: Motor Neuron Disease -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Younger onset, slowly progressive paraplegia, dorsal column involvement, sphincter dysfunction, may include symptoms of ataxia or dementia, especially with a positive family history (autosomal dominant). Forty percent have mutations in the SPAST gene; minimal upper limb involvement does not include severe corticobulbar involvement. ii) Primary progressive multiple sclerosis [29] Slowly progressive, positive CSF findings (oligoclonal bands) and demyelination. iii) Metabolic myelopathies [29] Vitamin B12 and copper associated myelopathies, slowly progressive myelopathy, typically associated with sensory neuropathy, and adult-onset slowly progressive myelopathy, iv) X linked adrenoleucodystrophy [29]

retrieved_docs.title: Subacute Combined Degeneration of the Spinal Cord(Archived) -- Evaluation -- Identify Demyelinating Lesions
retrieved_docs.content: In the early stages of the disease, demyelination is observed in the spine MRI as bilateral paired regions of T2 hyperintensity in the dorsal columns of the cervical and upper thoracic spinal cord. This finding is often called the "Inverted V" or "Inverted Rabbit Ears" sign. In later stages, T2 hyperintensity may also be seen in the lateral columns of the spinal cord. A decreased T1-weighted signal and contrast enhancement of the same regions may also be observed. Prompt treatment can improve signal abnormalities seen on the MRI. [28]

retrieved_docs.title: Subacute Combined Degeneration of the Spinal Cord(Archived) -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: Demyelinating lesions initially appear in the center of the dorsal columns of the upper thoracic cord. The lesions then spread laterally to lateral corticospinal tracts and cranially, involving the cervical cord and medulla. As demyelination and vacuolation progress, axons begin to undergo degeneration. In the late stages of the disease, dense gliosis occurs. Electron microscopic changes include the separation of myelin lamellae and the formation of intra-myelin vacuoles in the dorsal and lateral columns of the spinal cord.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 2582
retrieved_docs.content: Other disorders of the nervous system constitute a broad diagnostic category that includes a variety of specific neuropathic conditions. Within this category are “تكزز,” diabetic neuropathy (اعتلال الأعصاب السكري), idiopathic progressive neuropathy (اعتلال الأعصاب المترقي مجهول السبب), multiple neuropathy associated with malignant disease (اعتلال الأعصاب المتعدد في المرض الخبيث), severe neuropathy (اعتلال الأعصاب بالغ الخطورة), chronic inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy with root involvement (اعتلال العصب والجذور المتعدد الالتهابي المزمن مزيل للميالين), mononeuritis multiplex (التهاب العصب الأحادي المتعدد), and lower‑limb reflex sympathetic atrophy (الحثل الانعكاسي الودى للطرف السفلي). By classifying these diverse entities under the umbrella of “Other disorders of nervous system,” clinicians can recognize that they share a common neuro‑anatomical focus while differing in etiology, severity, and clinical presentation, which aids in systematic evaluation, differential diagnosis, and tailored patient management.

retrieved_docs.content: diseases, genetic neuropathies, and distinct syndromes. This unified structure supports coding, diagnosis, and patient‑centered management of a broad array of neurological conditions, emphasizing the central role of peripheral nerves, pain, paralysis, and targeted surgical decompression in clinical practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 3 -- Community 2228
retrieved_docs.content: Other disorders of nervous system is a broad diagnostic category that encompasses a diverse group of conditions, including the entities تكزز, اعتلال الأعصاب المترقي مجهول السبب, اعتلال الأعصاب المتعدد في المرض الخبيث, اعتلال عصبي عضلي, التهاب الشعب الهوائية الحاد المعدي, الحثل الانعكاسي الودى للطرف السفلي, داء شاركو‑ماري‑توث, and متلازمة ذنب الفرس مع مثانة عصبية. Each of these entities is classified under Other disorders of nervous system, reflecting their shared involvement of the nervous system despite differing pathophysiological mechanisms. For example, اعتلال الأعصاب المترقي مجهول السبب and اعتلال الأعصاب المتعدد في المرض الخبيث represent distinct forms of neuropathy, while اعتلال عصبي عضلي denotes a neuromuscular disorder. Inflammatory respiratory involvement is captured by التهاب الشعب الهوائية الحاد المعدي, whereas الحثل الانعكاسي الودى للطرف السفلي describes a reflexive degenerative process affecting the lower limb. Dاء شاركو‑ماري‑توث refers to a specific neurodegenerative disease, and متلازمة ذنب الفرس مع مثانة عصبية characterizes a syndrome combining spinal cord injury–related motor deficits with neurogenic bladder dysfunction. Recognizing that all these conditions are grouped under Other disorders of nervous system aids clinicians in systematic evaluation, differential diagnosis, and coordinated management of patients presenting with complex neurological presentations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 1 -- Community 250
retrieved_docs.content: illustrating that cauda equina or spinal pathologies can manifest as peripheral neuropathy. Structural foot abnormalities, exemplified by valgus malformation, are associated with lesions of the plantar nerve, underscoring the impact of musculoskeletal deformities on peripheral nerve health. Collectively, these relationships emphasize that peripheral nerves constitute a central anatomic substrate for a wide spectrum of metabolic, inflammatory, toxic, neoplastic, autoimmune and compressive neuropathies, and that targeted decompressive or incisional procedures at the nerve level are integral to the management of many of these disorders.

This framework supports precise anatomical localization, differential diagnosis, and therapeutic planning, especially the central role of nerve decompression procedures across multiple anatomical sites.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2239
retrieved_docs.content: Hemorrhage into nerve trunk, inflammatory and toxic neuropathy, and hereditary sensory neuropathy (اعتلال الأعصاب الحسي الوراثي) are all conditions in which the nerve serves as the primary site of pathological findings. This highlights that the nerve is a common locus for diverse neuropathic processes, ranging from acute hemorrhagic injury to inflammatory, toxic, and genetic sensory neuropathies. Recognizing the nerve as the affected structure helps clinicians focus diagnostic evaluation and tailor management strategies for patients presenting with these nerve‑related disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 3 -- Community 2390
retrieved_docs.content: Other disorders of nervous system is a diagnostic category that comprises a diverse group of neuropathic and inflammatory conditions. Within this category are the entities تكزز, اعتلال الأعصاب المترقي مجهول السبب (idiopathic progressive neuropathy), اعتلال الأعصاب المتعدد الكحولي (multifocal alcoholic neuropathy), اعتلال الأعصاب بالغ الخطورة (severe neuropathy), اعتلال العصب والجذور المتعدد الالتهابي المزمن مزيل للميالين (chronic multifocal inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy), التهاب العصب الأحادي المتعدد (multifocal mononeuritis), الحثل الانعكاسي الودى للطرف السفلي (reflexive atrophic changes of the lower limb), and وهن عضلي وبيل (muscular and peripheral weakness). Together, these conditions illustrate the breadth of disorders classified under other nervous system diseases, highlighting variations in etiology, severity, and anatomical distribution that are important for accurate diagnosis and targeted patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1437
retrieved_docs.content: The condition تمزق جافوي is closely associated with the dura mater, as the dura mater serves as the primary site where findings of تمزق جافوي are observed. In addition, تمزق جافوي functions as an antecedent or parent condition for spinal cord injury, indicating that the presence of تمزق جافوي may predispose patients to subsequent spinal cord injury. Recognizing the involvement of the dura mater in تمزق جافوي and its potential role in the development of spinal cord injury is important for accurate diagnosis, monitoring, and management of affected individuals.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 3 -- Community 2180
retrieved_docs.content: "Other disorders of nervous system" is a diagnostic category that encompasses a diverse group of peripheral nerve conditions. Within this category are the entities تكزز, اعتلال الأعصاب المترقي مجهول السبب (idiopathic progressive neuropathy), اعتلال الأعصاب المتعدد الكحولي (alcoholic polyneuropathy), اعتلال الأعصاب المتعدد في المرض الخبيث (polyneuropathy associated with malignant disease), اعتلال العصب والجذور المتعدد الالتهابي المزمن مزيل للميالين (chronic inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy), التهاب العصب الأحادي المتعدد (multifocal mononeuritis), الحثل الانعكاسي الودى للطرف السفلي (reflex sympathetic atrophy of the lower limb), and داء شاركو- ماري- توث (Charcot‑Marie‑Tooth disease). These conditions share common features of peripheral nerve involvement, ranging from metabolic or toxic injury (as in alcoholic polyneuropathy) to immune‑mediated demyelination (as in chronic inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy) and hereditary axonal degeneration (as in Charcot‑Marie‑Tooth disease). Recognizing that they are classified together under "Other disorders of nervous system" helps clinicians differentiate them from central nervous system diseases and guides appropriate diagnostic work‑up and therapeutic strategies tailored to the underlying pathophysiology of each disorder.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 930
retrieved_docs.content: Cerebral arteriopathy, autosomal dominant, with subcortical infarcts and leukoencephalopathy, type 1 is a hereditary cerebrovascular disorder that characteristically presents with a gait disorder (اضطراب المِشْيَة). The disease’s underlying vascular pathology leads to subcortical infarctions and diffuse white‑matter changes, which together disrupt motor pathways and result in abnormal walking patterns. Recognizing gait disturbance as a key manifestation of this specific type of cerebral arteriopathy aids clinicians in diagnosing the condition and guiding appropriate genetic counseling and management strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 2 -- Community 1239
retrieved_docs.content: Hemorrhage into nerve trunk, inflammatory and toxic neuropathy, and the hereditary sensory neuropathy (اعتلال الأعصاب الحسي الوراثي) are all clinical entities that have the nerve as their finding site, indicating that pathological changes in these conditions are localized to nerve tissue. Likewise, an incision of nerve is a direct procedural intervention performed on the nerve, underscoring the nerve as a common target for both disease processes and surgical treatment. Recognizing that the nerve serves as the anatomical locus for these diverse findings and procedures helps clinicians accurately diagnose neuropathic conditions, plan appropriate interventions, and coordinate patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 881
retrieved_docs.content: Laminectomy; excision intervertebral disc procedures are associated with the development of post‑laminectomy syndrome (متلازمة ما بعد استئصال الصفيحة الفقرية). Post‑laminectomy syndrome is classified as a subtype of spine disorder (اضطراب العمود الفقري). This relationship highlights that patients undergoing laminectomy or disc excision may experience persistent pain and functional impairment characteristic of this spinal disorder, underscoring the need for careful postoperative management and targeted therapeutic strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 2411
retrieved_docs.content: The category “Other disorders of nervous system” serves as an umbrella classification for a diverse group of neuropathic conditions. It includes polyneuropathy that results from medication use, multiple‑nerve‑bundle neuropathies that occur in malignant diseases, and similar multifocal neuropathies associated with collagen‑vascular disorders. Severe or high‑risk neuropathies are also placed within this group, as are chronic inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathies that involve both nerves and roots and lead to demyelination. Mononeuritis, a single‑nerve inflammation, and lower‑limb reflex sympathetic dystrophy (a painful, reflex‑driven condition of the extremities) are likewise classified under “Other disorders of nervous system.” Additionally, the term “تكزز” is listed as a subclass within this broader category, reflecting the inclusion of various language‑specific descriptors for these neurological disorders. Together, these classifications highlight the wide spectrum of peripheral and central nervous system pathologies that are grouped under “Other disorders of nervous system,” aiding clinicians in diagnosis, coding, and management of these heterogeneous conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2508
retrieved_docs.content: "Other disorders of nervous system" is a diagnostic category that encompasses a diverse group of peripheral nerve and neuromuscular conditions. Within this category are diabetic neuropathy (اعتلال الأعصاب السكري), a metabolic nerve damage caused by chronic hyperglycemia; multiple neuropathy associated with malignant disease (اعتلال الأعصاب المتعدد في المرض الخبيث), which reflects nerve involvement secondary to cancer; chronic inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy with root involvement (اعتلال العصب والجذور المتعدد الالتهابي المزمن مزيل للميالين), an immune‑mediated disorder characterized by demyelination of peripheral nerves; mononeuritis (التهاب العصب الأحادي) and its multiple form (التهاب العصب الأحادي المتعدد), inflammatory processes that affect a single nerve or several individual nerves; lower‑limb reflex atrophy (الحثل الانعكاسي الودى للطرف السفلي), a pattern of muscle wasting and diminished reflexes in the legs; and Charcot‑Marie‑Tooth disease (داء شاركو‑ماري‑توث), a hereditary motor‑sensory neuropathy. An additional entry labeled "تكزز" is also classified under this group, though its specific meaning is unclear. Collectively, these entities illustrate that "Other disorders of nervous system" includes metabolic, neoplastic, inflammatory, hereditary, and idiopathic neuropathies, highlighting the importance of recognizing varied etiologies when evaluating peripheral nervous system pathology.
